title: East Asia
subtitle: China, Japan, Korea — from terracotta armies to woodblock waves.
intro: East Asian art has a long memory. Forms travel — from Chinese brushwork to Japanese ukiyo-e, from Tang Buddhism to Korean celadon — and each reinvention carries the earlier one inside it.

works.title: Terracotta Army
works.desc: Over 8,000 soldiers, each with a different face, buried with the first emperor of China to guard him after death. Farmers digging a well found them in 1974.

works.title: Great Wall of China
works.desc: Not one wall but many, rebuilt across dynasties. The Ming sections visitors see today are a fraction of a system that once stretched over 13,000 miles.

works.title: Mogao Caves (Dunhuang)
works.desc: 492 caves carved into cliff face, filled with murals and gilded clay Buddhas. One sealed chamber held 50,000 manuscripts — the largest trove of its kind.

works.title: Hōryū-ji
works.meta: Asuka period · c. 607 CE · Nara, Japan
works.desc: Among the oldest wooden buildings still standing anywhere in the world. Built when Buddhism had only recently reached Japan.

works.title: Goryeo Celadon
works.desc: Stoneware with a jade-green glaze so admired that Song-dynasty Chinese called Korean celadon the "first under heaven."

works.title: Along the River During Qingming
works.desc: A five-meter handscroll showing hundreds of people in the Song capital on a spring festival day. Read right to left — it unfolds as you walk past.

works.title: Forbidden City
works.desc: 980 buildings on 180 acres. For five centuries, commoners who entered uninvited were executed. It is now the world's most-visited museum.

works.title: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
works.desc: A retirement villa turned Zen temple, its top two floors wrapped in gold leaf. Burned down by a young monk in 1950 — rebuilt in facsimile.

works.title: Ryōan-ji Rock Garden
works.desc: Fifteen stones in raked gravel. From no single vantage can you see all fifteen at once — you're always one short of a complete view.

works.title: Himeji Castle
works.desc: The finest surviving Japanese castle, nicknamed "White Heron" for its white plaster. Its winding approach was designed to confuse besiegers.

works.title: The Great Wave off Kanagawa
works.desc: A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.
